text: The surgeon holds a cautery scissor in the right hand and a fenestrated Maryland bipolar in the left hand, with a ProGrasp instrument in the fourth arm. The ROSI catheter, a robotic operated suction irrigation device, is visible in the surgical field.

The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg, with the camera port inserted at the umbilicus. The scissor port is located in the right lower quadrant at the umbilical level. An 8mm air seal assistant port is placed between the scissor arm on the right side and the camera port.

The surgeon releases the bowel to prevent injury during instrument changes and to avoid inadvertent burning. The colon is carefully released as the bladder is dropped, preparing for a posterior dissection of the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon makes an initial incision approximately one centimeter above the line between the white peritoneal reflection on the bladder and the yellow perirectal fat. The distinct line between these two tissues is visible, with the yellow fat at the bottom of the screen and the white peritoneal reflection at the top. The incision reveals the seminal vesicles and vas deferens, which are positioned flat and closer to the rectum, rather than off to the side, due to the approach taken.

After making the incision in the peritoneum, the surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to lift the vas deferens, revealing the seminal vesicle below, closer to the rectum. The medial dissection is performed first, followed by repositioning the fourth arm to visualize the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicle. The surgeon ensures the tip of the seminal vesicle is free before proceeding with lateral dissection.

The surgeon lifts the seminal vesicle (SV) straight up, then maneuvers around its tip to access the contralateral shoulder, revealing the lateral aspect of the SV. The surgeon adjusts retraction using the fourth arm to pull further on the SV, ensuring it emerges from the surrounding tissue. The dissection progresses along the lateral aspect of the SV, with close adherence to the structure.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to carefully peel tissue away from the seminal vesicles, ensuring not to veer too wide and avoid nerve damage. The seminal vesicles are dissected cleanly without any residual tissue, highlighting the precision of the bipolar technique.

The surgeon uses a cautery set at twenty-five for both monopolar and bipolar modes, carefully dissecting around the seminal vesicle. On the medial aspect, cautery is applied without concern for nerve injury, while on the lateral aspect, the surgeon closely hugs the seminal vesicle to avoid damaging nearby nerves. Between the seminal vesicles and the prostate, cautery is safely used on the anterior top side.
The surgeon exposes the vas deferens from the posterior side, revealing the anatomical orientation of the vas and seminal vesicles in this approach.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to lift the vas deferens straight up, providing counter traction to stabilize the seminal vesicle for dissection. The medial aspect of the seminal vesicle is freed, and the dissection progresses to the tip. The fourth arm is then repositioned to the contralateral shoulder to expose the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicle. The surgeon carefully hugs the seminal vesicle, peeling vessels away from its surface to avoid nerve injury, while adjusting the fourth arm to maintain optimal exposure.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the tip of the seminal vesicle, using bipolar cautery set at 25 to minimize thermal spread. Vessels are taken over the seminal vesicle and peeled away, avoiding cauterization far from the structure to prevent nerve injury.

The surgeon carefully dissects the tip of the seminal vesicle (SV), transitioning to a position that reveals the lateral aspect. The SV is hugged closely to avoid veering wide and preserve the nerves along its lateral border. Bipolar cautery is used to buzz and peel vessels off the SV, with the fourth arm providing counter traction to maintain exposure. The vas deferens is visible, and the surgeon waits to cut it until the lateral aspect of the SV is fully exposed.

The surgeon cuts the vas deferens along the edge of the dissected seminal vesicle, ensuring both ends retract and stay clear of the lateral edge of the seminal vesicle. The dissection continues upwards towards the prostate, with the seminal vesicle tip mobilized for easier access through the posterior bladder neck.

The surgeon mobilizes the tip of the seminal vesicle (SV) and some lateral tissue for easier lifting after bladder entry. The posterior leaflet of Denonvilliers' fascia is visible, with the incision made about a centimeter above the yellow periorectal fat to avoid rectal injury. The incision is kept narrow to prevent ureteral injury, ensuring the ureters remain unseen.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the seminal vesicles and proceeds to drop the bladder to enter the space of Retzius. An incision is made to the left and right of the ligaments to locate the pubic bone, followed by a midline incision to drop the dome of the bladder, ensuring safe navigation and avoiding injury to the bladder or suprapubic fat pad.

The surgeon makes an incision to the right of the ligament, carrying it down to expose the pubic bone. Blunt dissection is used to find the natural plane, avoiding excessive perivascular fat and ensuring the node packet is not inadvertently moved medially. The plane appears thin and cobweb-like, facilitating separation from the lateral sidewall and nodes.

The surgeon identifies a plane that is slightly adhered in the corner due to a small hernia. The hernia sac is visible, and the surgeon notes the importance of repairing any identified inguinal hernias, especially direct ones, to prevent potential bowel obstruction after the bladder is made intraperitoneal.

The surgeon identifies the pubic bone after making incisions on both sides and connects the incisions, ensuring the correct anatomical plane is maintained. The pubic bone serves as a landmark to avoid anterior entry into the rectus or posterior entry into the dorsal venous complex (DVC).

The surgeon continues dissection within the cobweb-like plane, using cautery to address small bleeders. The bladder is dropped, revealing the plane between the nodes and perivascular fat. The lateral peritoneal incision is extended down to the vas, ensuring the bladder can be mobilized without tension for later anastomosis. A small hernia is noted where bladder fat protrudes, and the surgeon carefully separates the nodes from the perivascular fat to avoid interference with node dissection.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to address an adherent area, revealing prominent lymph nodes on the left side. Fat is observed protruding into the obturator canal, identified as an obturator hernia. The surgeon carefully reduces or divides the herniated fat to enhance mobility towards the base of the prostate, ensuring no bleeding occurs that could retract into the obturator canal.

The surgeon cleans up the fat surrounding the prostate, revealing the superficial venous complex. The fat is carefully dissected, sometimes sent as a specimen due to the presence of nodes, although it is rare for these nodes to contain cancer.
The surgeon uses electrocautery set at 40-40 to remove perivascular fat obscuring the view of the bladder neck, ensuring a clear field for the upcoming bladder neck dissection and node removal.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm for retraction while incising the endopelvic fascia to aid in identifying the bladder neck for dissection. The incision helps reveal the contour of the prostate at its base, facilitating the removal of the prostate during the prostatectomy.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to retract the bladder medially, revealing the obturator nerve in a thin patient. The developed plane between the perivascular fat and nodes helps define the node packet, with minimal fat surrounding the obturator nerve and vein.

The surgeon makes an incision over the vein, carefully dissecting tissue away from the vein to better define anatomical planes. Bipolar energy is used extensively to control bleeding, avoiding monopolar cautery to prevent damage to the vein. Clips are applied distal to the node of Cloquet and proximal to the obturator nerve under the external iliac vein to prevent lymphatic back bleeding.

The surgeon uses bipolar energy to seal lymphatics, minimizing lymphatic leaks and potential lymphoceles. A clip is placed on the lymphatics distal to the node of Cloquet, coming back from the leg, to further reduce lymphatic leakage. The procedure involves careful clipping and sealing of lymphatics using bipolar energy.

The surgeon carefully uses bipolar energy to seal the lymphatics, applying clips where necessary to prevent lymphoceles. The surgical field shows meticulous attention to sealing lymphatic vessels around the node packet, avoiding the use of monopolar cautery to reduce the risk of asymptomatic lymphoceles.

The surgeon uses bipolar energy to seal lymphatics around the node packet, performing an anatomic dissection. The dissection involves removing a zone of tissue, carefully hugging anatomical structures such as the obturator nerve and the pelvic sidewall muscle beneath the vein. The goal is to clean out the nodal basin without leaving excess fat or nodes.

The surgeon performs a limited node dissection, focusing on the iliac and obturator nodes, while occasionally extending to the internal iliac nodes. The dissection is conducted on the medial side, going deeper than usual to potentially include internal iliac nodes, which are often overlooked by others.

The surgeon applies a second clip to prevent backflow of lymphatics from the node packet being removed. Both the proximal and distal ends of the packet are clipped, and bipolar energy is used to seal lymphatics in between, minimizing the risk of lymphocele formation.

The surgeon uses cautery set at forty to manage thermal spread while dissecting deeper nodes beyond the obturator nerve. On the medial side, the internal artery is visible, and some internal iliac nodes are removed from the deeper medial aspect near the obturator nerve.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to retract the bladder medially, pushing it to the right side to clear the surgical field. The internal iliac vessels are visible as the surgeon works deeply beyond the obturator nerve, removing fat and tissue to access the internal iliac nodes.

The surgeon uses a rosy catheter to suction fatty fluid from the surgical field, improving visibility and contact with the cautery device during the node dissection. The procedure involves meticulous sealing of lymphatics to prevent lymphocele formation.

The surgeon inspects the underside of the vein to ensure no lymphatics are left behind after dissection, with clips applied to prevent lymphatic leakage. The procedure involves meticulous examination to avoid complications from large lymphatic vessels. The surgeon prepares to repeat the process on the opposite side before proceeding to the prostate dissection.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to retract the bladder to the right side of the screen. The initial step involves identifying the vein, while removing overlying fat tissue that could obstruct the view. The surgeon carefully dissects the fat, which contains visible lymphatics, to prevent postoperative leakage. The vein is exposed, and the surgeon performs an anatomic dissection by closely following the vein's wall and peeling away surrounding tissue.

The surgeon identifies anatomical structures and carefully dissects around them to remove the lymph node packet in one piece, maintaining anatomic integrity. The dissection technique involves hugging the structures closely to avoid fragmentation and ensure clear identification of the nodes.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to carefully seal the epigastric vessels, which are visible branching off the iliac vessels and running anterior to the abdominal wall. The camera view is slightly obscured due to a dirty lens.

The surgeon has defined the vein and cleaned off its anterior surface. The assistant switches out the clip, preparing for the placement of a Hemalox clip at the corner near the note of Cloquet. The surgeon uses a robotic clip applier to precisely position the clip, ensuring it is placed in the exact spot and direction needed, which is crucial for the upcoming clipping of the prostate pedicles.

The surgeon uses a robotic wristed instrument to place clips on the anterior surface of the vein, then proceeds to dissect the underside of the vein, extending the dissection to the pelvic sidewall to avoid leaving excess fat. The vein is retracted significantly during the procedure, demonstrating its collapsibility and distensibility.

The surgeon uses a scissor instrument to retract the vein, ensuring no damage is done to the vein itself. In the event of a small hole or tear in the vein, a robotic Hemalox clip is applied tangentially to repair it without constricting the vein's size.

The surgeon completes dissection of the medial aspect of the vein and approaches the obturator nerve, visible in the surgical field. Using bipolar cautery, the surgeon minimizes stimulation of the obturator nerve. The obturator artery is identified, running into the obturator canal, and may be sacrificed if necessary. A clip is placed on the proximal end of the lymph node packet near the corner where the nerve meets the iliac vein, with further dissection needed to thin the area.

The surgeon carefully dissects around a prominent lymph node, thinning the tissue to avoid clipping the node itself. The obturator artery is visible, and the obturator nerve is defined to prevent accidental clipping, which could lead to temporary leg adduction palsy. The surgeon prepares to place a clip on the thinned node area.

The surgeon uses a Hemalox clip to secure lymphatic tissue, carefully thinning out the area to avoid clipping the nerve. A laparoscopic instrument, the hemlock clip remover, is available for removing clips if necessary. The surgeon identifies the nerve to prevent accidental clipping while ensuring all lymphatics are captured.

The surgeon clips the internal nodes on the deep aspect of the obturator nerve, ensuring the nerve is not compromised. The surgical field becomes fluid-filled, affecting the bipolar instrument's effectiveness, prompting the potential use of suction to clear the area. The dissection targets nodes potentially involved in prostate cancer, located deep to the nerve.

The surgeon completes dissection of the internal iliac nodes, revealing the internal iliac artery. There is visible bleeding in the corner, which is managed using a laparoscopic sucker. The surgeon applies additional bipolar cautery to control bleeding, noting the absence of a clip placement in this instance.

The surgeon uses a robotic suction device to manage bleeding in a challenging corner of the surgical field. The device is applied directly to the bleeding site to hold pressure and stop the bleeding, then slowly retracted to reveal the source of the bleeding for further management.

The surgeon completes the node dissection and removes the anterior prostate fat, placing it in a bag to be set aside until the end of the procedure. The surgical team prepares to clean the scope for improved visibility before proceeding to dissect the bladder neck. The scope has been used at two times magnification to minimize fogging and splatter during the procedure.

The surgeon reduces the cautery setting to 25 to minimize tissue charring while dissecting the bladder neck. The incision reveals mostly white tissue with some char from cautery use. The surgeon distinguishes the longitudinal bladder neck muscle fibers from the spongy prostate tissue, ensuring the incision follows the contour of the prostate without penetrating it. The bladder is peeled off the anterior prostate using backward pressure, aiming to locate the urethra in the midline.

The surgeon carefully dissects the bladder neck, using the contour of the prostate to ensure correct positioning. The cautery is set low to visualize muscle fibers, with the left hand providing counter traction. The surgeon touches the tissue lightly with the cautery, allowing muscle fibers to split naturally. The urethra is lifted with the fourth arm towards the anterior abdominal wall to identify the posterior bladder neck. The incision begins at the corners to prevent fish-mouthing and maintain a tight bladder neck, sparing it when cancer risk allows.

The surgeon carefully releases the corners of the bladder neck from the prostate, ensuring the bladder neck remains tight by cutting at the six o'clock position last. The left hand maintains tension on the tissue while the monopolar cautery is used to split the longitudinal muscle fibers with minimal charring, confirming the correct anatomical location without risk of entering the prostate.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to lift the median lobe and releases the corners from either side, maintaining a tight bladder neck. The left hand provides stretch, clearly revealing the bladder neck fibers. The seminal vesicles, dissected earlier, are visible, aiding in identifying the correct dissection plane through the posterior bladder neck.

The surgeon completes the bladder neck dissection, revealing the vas deferens. The dissection continues on the posterior bladder neck, focusing on the corners at the base of the prostate. The surgeon removes bladder muscle fibers down to the perivascular fat, exposing some vessels. The bladder is fully released from the lateral aspects, preventing it from obstructing the view of the rectum.

The surgeon performs rectal dissection by making an incision in the posterior Denonvilliers fascia, entering the perirectal fat plane, and peeling the perirectal fat off the backside of the prostate while leaving a leaflet of Denonvilliers fascia on the prostate. This approach creates a mostly blunt dissection plane, providing a natural barrier for cancer margin on the posterior side.

The surgeon performs a high anterior release of the prostatic fascia, exposing the neurovascular bundle along the prostate. The incision is made high up to identify the plane and follow it down to the location of the main trunk of the neurovascular bundle. Veins are visible in the area, and the procedure is conducted at a pneumo pressure of six millimeters of mercury using the air seal system.

The surgeon uses a robotic clip applier to secure the pedicle close to the prostate, ensuring minimal tissue remains at the base to avoid injuring the neurovascular bundle. The neurovascular bundle is visible at the six o'clock position, turning posterolateral to the prostate, and is carefully peeled away from the gland.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection on the posterolateral aspect of the prostate gland, releasing the neurovascular bundle by making small incisions in the lateral edge of the Denonvilliers fascia. The majority of the dissection is blunt, with occasional cuts to separate the fascia, while the air seal system maintains pneumo pressure and minimizes venous bleeding.

The surgeon continues the procedure at a pneumo pressure of six, emphasizing the benefits of low pressure for patient recovery. The high anterior release on the prostatic fascia is visible, and the surgeon carefully positions the clip close to the prostate. The rectal dissection reveals the backside of the prostate, and the surgeon manipulates the pedicle to view the prostate's bottom edge.

The surgeon places a single clip at the base of the prostate capsule, ensuring proximity to the prostate to maximize nerve sparing. The clip is positioned precisely to avoid leaving excess tissue at the pedicle, maintaining the integrity of the neurovascular bundle below the prostate curve.

The surgeon is positioned on the prostate capsule, cutting the medial edge of Denonvilliers' fascia to release the neurovascular bundle. Blunt dissection is used to peel the neurovascular bundle off the prostate, following a high anterior release on the lateral aspect of the gland. The pedicle was taken in the correct location, allowing access to the appropriate plane for nerve-sparing dissection. The surgeon carefully extends the dissection to the apex of the gland to avoid injuring the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon cuts through the dorsal venous complex (DVC) without pre-placing a dorsal vein stitch or using a stapler, opting instead to over-sew it. This approach allows flexibility in cutting the DVC closer to the prostate in this lower-risk case. Despite the low pneumo pressure of six, the bleeding from the DVC is minimal and manageable, with the surgeon using bipolar cautery for arterial bleeding while allowing venous bleeding to occur naturally.

The surgeon cuts the urethra, revealing the catheter, and pulls it back to expose the posterior aspect of the urethra. The prostatectomy is completed with the final cut through the urethra, followed by an instrument change to needle drivers for over-sewing the DVC. The prostatectomy specimen shows successful nerve sparing, with minimal tissue left at the base near the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon places a figure-of-eight stitch using an ovicryl suture and a CT-one needle to secure the dorsal venous complex (DVC), ensuring the stitch remains medial to the neurovascular bundle. The procedure involves tying the knot and potentially adding additional throws if the DVC is larger than can be encompassed in one stitch. Despite some venous bleeding visible in the surgical field, the blood loss is minimal and does not compromise visibility.

The surgeon places additional sutures to incorporate the remaining dorsal venous complex (DVC) into the stitch, noting that there is no active bleeding. The puboprostatic ligaments are also included in the stitch. The surgeon then secures the DVC stitch through the pubic symphysis to compress it against the bone, aiming to prevent postoperative bleeding. A CT-1 needle with ovicryl suture is prepared for the Rocco stitch, cut to a standard length for consistency.

The surgeon uses a rosy catheter to suction blood clots for improved visibility during posterior reconstruction and anastomosis. The ovacral suture is prepared for use, ensuring it stays medial to the neurovascular bundle. The suture is placed horizontally under the urethra, incorporating the posterior plate and cut edge of Denonvilliers' fascia.

The surgeon incorporates the edge of the peritoneum into the closure to prevent any space behind the bladder, ensuring the posterior bladder is positioned away from the bladder neck. A slider knot is used to secure the tissues, reducing tension on the anastomosis and preventing bleeding from small arteries and venous sinuses behind the urethra. The Stratafix barbed suture is employed for the anastomosis, with a Pledge-It used to prevent the suture from pulling through, indicating the midpoint when tension is felt.

The surgeon begins the urethral anastomosis by placing the first stitch at the four or five o'clock position, inserting two stitches through the urethra and three through the bladder, then cinching it down. This technique completes the posterior aspect of the anastomosis, ensuring mucosa-to-mucosa approximation to prevent leaks. The posterior wall is clearly visible, confirming no potential for leakage.

The surgeon uses their left hand to identify the urethral edge and places a suture through the mucosa without the aid of a catheter. The suture is advanced up to the ten or eleven o'clock position to facilitate easier completion of the anterior anastomosis. The surgeon employs a suction catheter to clear the field, ensuring visibility of the mucosal edges on both the urethra and bladder for precise mucosal apposition, which is crucial to prevent postoperative leaks.

The surgeon completes the anterior portion of the anastomosis, ensuring mucosa-to-mucosa approximation to prevent urine leaks and inflammation. The use of barbed sutures enhances the watertight seal, reducing the need for a JP drain postoperatively. After finishing the anastomosis, the catheter is inserted, and the bladder is filled with saline to check for leaks.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis and inserts a new 18 French Foley catheter. The bladder is filled with saline to test the anastomosis, confirming no urine leak. The needles are removed, and the prostate is bagged, concluding the procedure. The patient is prepared for postoperative observation, with plans for same-day discharge.
